title: GROUP BY & Aggregates: From Rows to Answers
learningObjectives: Use aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) to summarize many rows into one answer
Group rows with GROUP BY to get one summary per category instead of one for the whole table
Filter grouped results with HAVING, distinct from filtering individual rows with WHERE
Avoid selecting a non-aggregated, non-grouped column alongside an aggregate
whyItMatters: 'Which substation had the highest average demand this month' isn't a question about any single row — it's a question about many rows summarized per substation. Aggregation is how the Digital Twin answers 'how are we doing overall,' not just 'show me one reading.'
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first real summary reporting — average, peak, and total demand per substation per day, the exact shape of report the Grid Copilot dashboard will eventually surface.

problem.summary: You can list rows, but not yet summarize them per substation.
problem.content: You can list every individual reading for substation-12, but you can't yet answer 'what was substation-12's average demand today' without doing the arithmetic yourself outside the database.

motivation.summary: Most operational questions are summary questions, not single-row lookups.
motivation.content: Nearly every operational question about the grid is really a summary question — average, peak, total — not a request for raw rows. Aggregation is the database doing that summarizing directly, correctly, and fast, even across millions of rows.

theory.summary: Aggregate functions summarize rows; GROUP BY produces one summary per category; HAVING filters after grouping.
theory.content: `AVG(demand_kw)`, `SUM(demand_kw)`, `COUNT(*)`, `MIN`, `MAX` compute one value across a set of rows. Without `GROUP BY`, an aggregate summarizes the entire result set into one row. `GROUP BY source_id` instead produces one summary row per distinct `source_id`. `HAVING` filters *after* grouping (e.g. `HAVING AVG(demand_kw) > 4500`), distinct from `WHERE`, which filters individual rows *before* grouping happens. Selecting a plain column alongside an aggregate without grouping by it is either an error or produces an arbitrary, meaningless value, depending on the database.

visualExplanation.summary: Rows cluster into colored groups, then collapse into one summary row per group.
visualExplanation.content: Rows cluster into colored groups by `source_id`, then each group collapses into a single summary row showing that group's average/max/count.

interactiveDemo.summary: Toggle a GROUP BY column and watch rows cluster and collapse live.
interactiveDemo.content: Toggle a GROUP BY column on a live table visualization and watch rows visually cluster into colored groups before collapsing into summary rows as the aggregate function is applied.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: aggregate, group, and filter grouped results.
guidedCoding.content:
```sql
SELECT source_id, AVG(demand_kw) AS avg_demand, MAX(demand_kw) AS peak_demand
FROM readings
GROUP BY source_id
HAVING AVG(demand_kw) > 4500
ORDER BY avg_demand DESC;
```

independentCoding.summary: Aggregate a per-source count and earliest-occurrence timestamp, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Write a query that returns, per source, the count of OVERLOAD-range readings and the earliest timestamp one occurred, for sources with more than 2 such readings.

debugging.summary: Fix a query mixing an ungrouped column with an aggregate.
debugging.content: A query selects `timestamp` alongside `AVG(demand_kw)` without grouping by `timestamp`, producing an error or a meaningless arbitrary value. Fix it by either grouping by the column appropriately or removing it.

reflection.summary: An aggregate collapses many rows — an ungrouped column has no single value to show.
reflection.content: Why does mixing an ungrouped column with an aggregate function not make sense, once you think about what value that column could possibly show for an entire group of rows?

teachBack.summary: Explain WHERE vs. HAVING to someone who assumes they're interchangeable.
teachBack.content: Explain the difference between WHERE and HAVING to someone who assumes they're interchangeable.

miniProject.summary: Build the Digital Twin's first daily summary report.
miniProject.content: Build the Digital Twin's first daily summary report: average, peak, and total demand per substation for the current day, using a single aggregated query.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This report is the backing implementation for the eventual Grid Copilot dashboard.
portfolioEvidence.content: This summary report is the exact shape of data the eventual Grid Copilot dashboard will display — this query is its first real backing implementation.

labs.title: Daily Summary
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise producing per-source aggregated summaries with HAVING filters, including a deliberately broken ungrouped-column query to fix.

simulations.title: GROUP BY Cluster Visualizer
simulations.description: Rows visually cluster into colored groups by a chosen column, then collapse into one summary row per group.

flashcards.front: What's the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
flashcards.back: WHERE filters individual rows before grouping happens; HAVING filters the summarized groups after GROUP BY has produced them.

flashcards.front: Why can't you SELECT a plain, ungrouped column alongside AVG(demand_kw) without grouping by it?
flashcards.back: Each group collapses to one row, but a plain column could have many different values across the rows in that group — there's no single correct value to show unless you group by it or aggregate it too.

rubric: Correctly explains why an ungrouped column can't sit alongside an aggregate without being grouped or aggregated
Correctly distinguishes WHERE (pre-grouping row filter) from HAVING (post-grouping group filter) with a working example of each
Daily summary report correctly computes average, peak, and total per substation
